title: How to Track Real Estate Deals and Expenses (Step-by-Step Guide)
excerpt: Tracking your real estate deals and expenses is the backbone of a profitable investing business. Learn the systems, tools, and habits that top investors use to stay organized and maximize returns.
author: AIWholesail Team
tags: how to track real estate deals and expenses
metaDescription: Learn how to track real estate deals and expenses with proven systems, tools, and templates. Stay organized, cut costs, and close more profitable deals.
metaKeywords: how to track real estate deals and expenses, real estate deal tracking, real estate expense tracking, wholesaling spreadsheet, real estate investing systems, deal pipeline management, real estate CRM

sections.content: Most real estate investors lose money not because they make bad deals — but because they have no idea where their money is going. They miss follow-ups, forget holding costs, and miscount their marketing spend. If you want to build a serious investing business, learning how to track real estate deals and expenses is just as important as knowing how to find motivated sellers. This skill becomes even more critical as you grow. If you're working toward scaling up, check out the [Scaling a Real Estate Investing Business: Complete Guide](https://aiwholesail.com/guides/scaling-real-estate-investing-business) — it covers the exact systems you'll need at every stage of growth.
Why Tracking Real Estate Deals and Expenses Matters

Imagine you closed 12 deals last year and grossed $180,000 in assignment fees. Sounds great, right? But after marketing costs, disposition fees, transaction coordinators, software subscriptions, driving-for-dollars mileage, and earnest money deposits, your actual profit might be closer to $90,000 — or less. Without proper tracking, you have no idea which marketing channels are working, which deal types are most profitable, or where you're bleeding cash. Real estate is a business, and businesses that don't track their numbers don't last.
What gets measured gets managed. In real estate investing, the investors who track every dollar are the ones still standing after five years.
Step 1: Build Your Deal Pipeline Tracker

Your deal pipeline is a live view of every lead and deal in your business at any given moment. Think of it like a sales funnel — leads come in at the top and either fall out or convert into closed deals at the bottom. You need to track the stage of every lead so nothing slips through the cracks.
Pipeline Stages to Track

items: New Lead — just came in from direct mail, cold calling, or online marketing
Contacted — you've reached the seller and had a conversation
Appointment Set — you have a walkthrough or phone appointment scheduled
Offer Made — you've submitted a written offer to the seller
Under Contract — signed purchase agreement in hand
In Due Diligence — inspections, title search, and contractor bids underway
Assigned or Listed — deal is being marketed to buyers or listed on MLS
Closed — deal is done, money is in your account
Dead — lead fell out at some stage (always note the reason why)

sections.content: Pro Tip: Review your pipeline every Monday morning. Spend 20 minutes updating deal stages, scheduling follow-ups, and flagging any deals that have been stuck in one stage for more than two weeks. Stalled deals usually mean missed profit.
Step 2: Track Every Deal's Numbers Before You Close

Every deal needs its own financial snapshot before you commit. That means calculating the After Repair Value (ARV), estimated rehab costs, maximum allowable offer (MAO), and your projected profit. If you're wholesaling, you also need to account for your assignment fee and what margin you're leaving for your cash buyer. A free [Wholesale Deal Calculator](https://aiwholesail.com/tools/wholesale-deal-calculator) can help you run these numbers in seconds so you're never guessing on a deal.
Key Numbers to Record for Every Deal

items: Property address and seller contact info
Lead source (direct mail, cold call, driving for dollars, etc.)
ARV based on comparable sales
Estimated repair cost from contractor walk or scope of work
Purchase price / contract price
Assignment fee or projected net profit
Closing date and any key deadlines
Earnest money deposit amount and who holds it
Buyer information if wholesaling

sections.content: Step 3: Set Up a Real Estate Expense Tracking System

Deal-level tracking is only half the picture. You also need a master expense log that captures every dollar leaving your business. Many investors are shocked to discover they're spending $3,000 to $5,000 per month on marketing, software, and overhead before accounting for any deal-related costs. A simple spreadsheet with expense categories — or a tool like QuickBooks or Wave — can make this painless if you stay consistent.
Common Real Estate Investing Expense Categories

items: Marketing: direct mail, PPC, Facebook ads, bandit signs, cold calling services
Transaction costs: title, closing fees, earnest money, transfer taxes
Holding costs (for flips or rentals): insurance, utilities, property taxes, loan interest
Rehab costs: broken down by labor and materials
Team and labor: VAs, acquisitions reps, disposition agents, contractors
Software and tools: CRM, skip tracing, dialer, e-signature platforms
Education: courses, masterminds, conferences
Professional services: attorney, CPA, bookkeeper
Travel and vehicle: mileage, gas, property visits

sections.content: Pro Tip: Open a dedicated business checking account and run every investing expense through it. This makes tax time dramatically easier and gives you an automatic paper trail of every dollar spent.
Step 4: Choose the Right Tracking Tools
You don't need a complex tech stack to track deals well. What you need is consistency. Here's a practical breakdown of tools based on your experience level.
Beginner: Spreadsheets

Google Sheets or Excel work perfectly when you're doing fewer than five deals a month. Build one sheet for your pipeline, one for deal financials, and one for monthly expenses. Keep it simple and update it daily. If you're just getting started, the [Real Estate Investing for Beginners](https://aiwholesail.com/guides/real-estate-investing-for-beginners) guide walks through the foundational systems you'll need before adding complexity.
Intermediate: CRM Software

Once you're managing 20 or more leads per month, a CRM becomes essential. Tools like REI Simple, Podio, InvestorFuse, or HubSpot let you track every lead, deal stage, communication history, and follow-up task in one place. If you're wholesaling, a purpose-built real estate CRM will save hours every week. You can even delegate pipeline management to a virtual assistant — see [Hiring a Virtual Assistant for Real Estate Investing](https://aiwholesail.com/blog/virtual-assistant-real-estate-investing) for a full breakdown of how to set that up.
Advanced: Full Business Dashboards

High-volume investors often build custom dashboards in Airtable or Notion, connected to their CRM and accounting software. These give a live view of KPIs like cost per lead, cost per deal, average days to close, and total pipeline value. At this stage, you'll also want to bring on an [acquisitions manager](https://aiwholesail.com/blog/acquisitions-manager-real-estate) to run the pipeline while you focus on strategy.
Step 5: Review Your Numbers Weekly and Monthly

Tracking means nothing if you never look at the data. Schedule a weekly 30-minute review of your deal pipeline and a monthly deep dive into your expenses and profitability. Ask yourself: Which lead sources are producing the most deals? What is my average cost per closed deal? Am I on track to hit my annual profit goal? These questions will quickly reveal where to double down and where to cut spending.
Monthly Metrics to Review

items: Total leads generated by source
Conversion rate from lead to contract
Number of deals closed
Gross revenue and net profit
Total marketing spend and cost per deal
Pipeline value (total projected profit of active deals)
Average days from contract to close

sections.content: Real-World Example: Tracking a Wholesale Deal

Let's say you're working a deal in [Atlanta, GA](https://aiwholesail.com/markets/atlanta-ga). The ARV is $220,000, rehab is estimated at $45,000, and you negotiate a contract price of $105,000. You market the deal and assign the contract to a cash buyer for $120,000, netting a $15,000 assignment fee. Your deal tracker would log: lead source (direct mail campaign, cost $1,200), marketing time (18 days from lead to contract), contract price, assignment price, closing date, and net profit. After subtracting a proportionate share of your monthly overhead — say $800 — your true net on this deal is around $14,200. That level of detail is what separates profitable investors from busy ones.

Investors in competitive markets like [Dallas, TX](https://aiwholesail.com/markets/dallas-tx) and [Phoenix, AZ](https://aiwholesail.com/markets/phoenix-az) are running these exact numbers on every deal to stay competitive. Tighter margins in hot markets make accurate tracking non-negotiable. If you're newer to the wholesale model, the [Wholesaling Real Estate for Beginners](https://aiwholesail.com/guides/wholesaling-real-estate-for-beginners) guide covers the full process from finding deals to closing.
Delegating Deal Tracking as You Scale

As your business grows, you'll want to delegate the day-to-day tracking to your team. A virtual assistant can update your pipeline, log expenses, and prepare weekly reports. An acquisitions manager should own the deal pipeline and be accountable for conversion metrics. Learn how to build the right [real estate investing team structure](https://aiwholesail.com/blog/real-estate-investing-team-structure) so every person knows exactly what they're responsible for tracking.
